coredata.dc:title: Enhanced hydrogen production from sucrose-derived carbon quantum dots-supported Ru catalysts: A comparative study of KBH4 and NaBH4 hydrolysis

coredata.dc:description: Despite increasing interest in borohydride hydrolysis for hydrogen generation, limited studies have explored carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from low-cost, renewable sources such as sucrose as catalyst supports. In this study, sucrose-derived CQDs were synthesized via a green hydrothermal method and employed as a support material for Ru catalysts. A novel hydrogen potential catalytic activity (HPCA) metric was introduced to compare the performance of Ru@CQDs in the hydrolysis of sodium borohydride (SBH) and potassium borohydride (PBH), addressing the lack of normalized efficiency metrics across different hydrogen sources. The ideal conditions for each hydrolysis reaction were established based on the hydrogen generation rate (HGR) after studying various catalyst characteristics using UV–Vis, UV-PL, TEM, and FT-IR analyses. The HGR values using 4 % NaBH4 and 4 % KBH4 were 167,588 and 122,527 mL g−1 min−1, respectively. However, the hydrogen potential catalytic activity metrics (HPCA), calculated based on the hydrogen content of the sources, were 1.551,741 mL g−1 min−1 and 1.655,770 mL g−1 min−1 for NaBH4 and KBH4 hydrolysis, respectively. This indicates that the rate of hydrogen production during KBH4 hydrolysis is higher than that during NaBH4 hydrolysis. For each hydrolysis reaction, the catalyst's turnover frequency (TOF) was also determined to assess its effectiveness: 924.8 mol H2.mol. Ru−1.min−1 for NaBH4 hydrolysis and 573.3 mol H2.mol Ru−1.min−1 for KBH4 hydrolysis. Additionally, the experimental data on the kinetics and activation energy of the hydrolysis processes, as well as the catalyst's reusability, were analyzed.

Ru metal may reduce the surface energy of CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the Scientific Research Projects Coordination Office of Siirt University with the project number of 2022-S\u0130\u00DCFEB-010 .

By using TEM (Hitachi High Tech HT7700) and FTIR spectra (Nicolet 520), respectively, the catalyst's surface characteristics and surfactant groups were identified. ICP-OES (Agilent 7800) analysis was used to determine the Ru component of the supported catalyst composite material. Additionally, UV\u2013Vis fluorescence analysis was performed to ascertain the photoluminescence properties of the synthesized CQDs.The TEM pictures of the Suc-CQDs and the Ru@Suc-CQDs (in pure water, 50 nm) are displayed in Figs. 2 and 3. The TEM picture provides insight into the catalyst's homogenous structure. There is an important factor that affects the active and efficient nature of supported catalysts, which is the size of the metal particles [46]. The average particle size of sucrose-derived CQDs was 17.2 nm, while that of Ru@CQDs decreased significantly to 4.3 nm. Several factors may explain this reduction: (i) Ru metal may influence the growth kinetics of CQDs, leading to slower growth and smaller final particle sizes; (ii) Ru atoms can stabilize smaller particles by interacting strongly with carbon atoms, thus preventing aggregation; (iii) Ru metal may reduce the surface energy of CQDs, enhancing the formation of smaller, more stable nanoparticles; (iv) Ru atoms may act as a passivating layer, inhibiting further growth of the carbon dots; and (v) Ru metal inclusion has the potential to cause lattice strain in CQDs, which could also contribute to the formation of smaller particles. Nurunnabi and Turn [47] demonstrated that metal additives, including Ru, can alter pore structures and strain in supported catalysts, leading to changes in particle size. Li et al. [48] showed that incorporating CQDs into Ru-based electrocatalysts improved hydrogen evolution by facilitating the formation of smaller, well-dispersed nanoparticles. Mozaffari et al. [49] provided kinetic modeling evidence showing how metal\u2013ligand interactions can regulate nanoparticle nucleation and growth, thus controlling size. Similarly, Li et al. [50] developed carbon quantum dot-loaded Ru nanoparticles and attributed their high catalytic activity to the reduced particle size and uniform dispersion achieved through CQD support.This study was supported by the Scientific Research Projects Coordination Office of Siirt University with the project number of 2022-S\u0130\u00DCFEB-010.

head.ce:title: Enhanced hydrogen production from sucrose-derived carbon quantum dots-supported Ru catalysts: A comparative study of KBH4 and NaBH4 hydrolysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite increasing interest in borohydride hydrolysis for hydrogen generation, limited studies have explored carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from low-cost, renewable sources such as sucrose as catalyst supports. In this study, sucrose-derived CQDs were synthesized via a green hydrothermal method and employed as a support material for Ru catalysts. A novel hydrogen potential catalytic activity (HPCA) metric was introduced to compare the performance of Ru@CQDs in the hydrolysis of sodium borohydride (SBH) and potassium borohydride (PBH), addressing the lack of normalized efficiency metrics across different hydrogen sources. The ideal conditions for each hydrolysis reaction were established based on the hydrogen generation rate (HGR) after studying various catalyst characteristics using UV–Vis, UV-PL, TEM, and FT-IR analyses. The HGR values using 4 % NaBH4 and 4 % KBH4 were 167,588 and 122,527 mL g−1 min−1, respectively. However, the hydrogen potential catalytic activity metrics (HPCA), calculated based on the hydrogen content of the sources, were 1.551,741 mL g−1 min−1 and 1.655,770 mL g−1 min−1 for NaBH4 and KBH4 hydrolysis, respectively. This indicates that the rate of hydrogen production during KBH4 hydrolysis is higher than that during NaBH4 hydrolysis. For each hydrolysis reaction, the catalyst's turnover frequency (TOF) was also determined to assess its effectiveness: 924.8 mol H2.mol. Ru−1.min−1 for NaBH4 hydrolysis and 573.3 mol H2.mol Ru−1.min−1 for KBH4 hydrolysis. Additionally, the experimental data on the kinetics and activation energy of the hydrolysis processes, as well as the catalyst's reusability, were analyzed.

ce:section.ce:para: The challenges posed by the depletion of hydrocarbon fuel resources are further compounded due to global climate change caused by increased energy consumption and CO2 emissions from fossil fuels [1]. Therefore, there is an increasing need to adopt innovative, renewable, low-carbon energy sources. Hydrogen is an ecologically safe energy source that offers significant advantages for supporting a possible green energy economy because of its high density of energy and harmless byproducts [2,3]. When used as an energy carrier, two crucial factors come into play: its high storage capacity and adjustable release rate [4,5].

SBH holds a significant position among hydrogen storage materials due to its tiny volume and low weight, stable structure, and storability in addition to having a significant capacity of hydrogen storage (10.8 %). It is also known for its exothermic self-reaction and the release of final products that are healthier for the environment [6,7]. The hydrolysis of NaBH4 proceeds as follows (Equ. 1):
(1)

Another borohydride with favorable hydrolytic properties and a high hydrogen content (7.4 %) by gravity is PBH. The lack of research on KBH4 hydrolysis producing hydrogen is most likely due to the higher production costs and lower gravimetric hydrogen concentration of KBH4 than NaBH4. During the hydrolysis process, KBH4 generates less heat than NaBH4 [8–10]. The equation below (Equ. 2) describes the hydrolysis of KBH4.
(2)
K B
H 4
+ 2
H
2
O
→ K B
O
2
+ 4
H 2

However, both processes require an efficient catalyst to proceed effectively, prompting researchers to develop a wide range of homogeneous and heterogeneous systems. İzgi [11] demonstrated that Co-B-Cr catalysts activated by microwave treatment significantly improved hydrogen generation from NaBH4 hydrolysis. Sahin et al. [12] investigated the effect of using ethanol instead of water in the preparation of Ni@CQDs catalysts and showed that the catalytic activity increased especially after solvent exchange. In another study, Onat et al. [13] reported the high hydrogen production rate via sodium borohydride hydrolysis with Co catalyst decorated with CQDs-doped GO. Lin and Chang [14] synthesized a magnetic cobalt/carbon composite derived from a zeolitic imidazolate framework, achieving efficient hydrogen production due to high surface area and magnetic recyclability.

It is well established that transition metals play a crucial role in catalyzing the hydrolysis of both sodium and potassium borohydrides. Xu et al. [15] reported that Pt/C catalysts significantly enhance hydrogen production from alkaline NaBH4 solutions due to their high surface activity. Patel et al. [16] demonstrated the effectiveness of Pd-C powders and thin films, showing rapid hydrogen release under ambient conditions. Huang et al. [17] employed Ru catalysts for NaBH4 hydrolysis and observed high catalytic efficiency with good reaction stability. Şahin et al. [18] developed a novel palladium complex catalyst that showed strong performance in terms of both hydrogen generation rate and catalyst reusability. Arzac and Fernández [19] highlighted the advantages of PVD-based techniques for preparing metal catalysts, particularly emphasizing their controlled structure and improved dispersion for efficient borohydride hydrolysis. Various transition metals have been investigated for their catalytic activity in borohydride hydrolysis. For instance, Fe-based catalysts have demonstrated good reusability and affordability for large-scale hydrogen production [20]. Ni catalysts, though less active than noble metals, offer a cost-effective alternative with moderate hydrogen generation efficiency [21]. Cu has been employed due to its electronic properties and synergistic effects when alloyed with other metals [22]. Rh and Ru catalysts exhibit excellent catalytic performance due to their high hydrogen dissociation activity [23,24]. Pd- and Pt-based systems have also been extensively studied for their superior kinetics and durability under harsh conditions [25,26], while Au nanoparticles have shown promise due to their surface plasmon resonance-enhanced reactivity in alkaline environments [27].

Support materials are essential for enhancing catalyst stability and ensuring uniform metal dispersion across high surface area substrates. Choy et al. [28] demonstrated that nanocrystalline sodalite structures formed via solid–solid transformation from Al2O3-pillared clay can offer structural robustness and high surface reactivity, making them suitable as support materials. Hagen [29] discussed the role of electronic and structural interactions—such as van der Waals forces, electron transfer, and adhesion—between catalysts and supports in industrial catalysis. Additionally, Li et al. [30] showed that titanate nanotube-supported Co catalysts exhibited improved hydrogen generation from NaBH4 hydrolysis due to enhanced dispersion and strong metal–support interactions.

Carbon-based nanomaterials known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have small spherical shapes around 10 nm in diameter and functional groups on their surfaces [31]. Carbon dots have attracted significant attention due to their carbon skeleton structure, which enhances metal dispersion and catalytic efficiency. Their excellent water solubility, low toxicity, tunable fluorescence, and high quantum yield make them highly suitable for catalytic and sensing applications. Meng et al. [32] highlighted the versatility of biomass-derived carbon dots, emphasizing their eco-friendly synthesis and high performance in energy-related applications. Mohammadinejad et al. [33] provided a broad overview of plant-derived nanostructures, including carbon dots, focusing on their biocompatibility and green chemistry potential. Atchudan et al. [34] synthesized hydrophilic, nitrogen-doped carbon dots from banana peels and demonstrated their applicability in both environmental remediation and biological imaging, owing to their strong fluorescence and surface functionality. CQDs have been shown to enhance the catalytic activity of metal nanoparticles, especially when integrated into solid-phase carriers. Wang et al. [2] demonstrated that incorporating CQDs into metal catalysts improved electron transfer and stability in hydrogen evolution reactions. Zhu et al. [35] reported that doping CQDs into porous supports enhanced surface functionality and catalytic dispersion. Similarly, Mou et al. [36] observed that CQD-modified carriers contributed to improved metal anchoring and resistance to agglomeration under reaction conditions. Other common methods for producing CQDs include laser ablation [37], electrochemical exfoliation [38], chemical oxidation [39], microwave-assisted synthesis [40], and ultrasonic treatment [41]. Among these, the hydrothermal method is widely preferred due to its simplicity, green chemistry compatibility, and its suitability for biomass-derived carbon sources like sucrose [42].

Sucrose is an affordable and sustainable substance widely used as a carbon source and energy supplier in the fermentation, food, and feed industries by various heterotrophic organisms [43,44]. It is a disaccharide composed of glucose and fructose and can be biosynthesized by cyanobacteria, algae, and plants [45]. This study marks the first use of sucrose in a novel application: the synthesis of carbon quantum dots.

In this work, by loading Ru particles on Sucrose-CQDs (Suc-CQDs) support, a unique catalytic system was developed that generates hydrogen from both NaBH4 and KBH4 hydrolysis. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study utilizing sucrose-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a support for ruthenium (Ru) catalysts in the catalytic hydrolysis of both SBH and PBH. The study introduces a new metric—Hydrogen Potential Catalytic Activity (HPCA)—that normalizes hydrogen generation performance based on the hydrogen content of the borohydride source. The comparative evaluation of reaction kinetics, thermodynamics, and catalyst reusability provides important insights into the design of efficient and eco-friendly hydrogen production systems. The creation of CQDs and the evolution of binding among CQDs and ions of metals were investigated using UV–Vis and PL spectrophotometers. Using several analytical techniques, the produced catalyst's characteristics are also clarified.

In this investigation, the following materials were used: NaBH4 (Merck), KBH4 (Acros), NaOH, KOH, Sucrose (Sigma-Aldrich), Ruthenium (III) chloride, deionized water. Sucrose, commonly referred to as tea sugar, is a disaccharide with the chemical formula C12H22O11 formed by the combination of one molecule each of glucose and fructose.

By using TEM (Hitachi High Tech HT7700) and FTIR spectra (Nicolet 520), respectively, the catalyst's surface characteristics and surfactant groups were identified. ICP-OES (Agilent 7800) analysis was used to determine the Ru component of the supported catalyst composite material. Additionally, UV–Vis fluorescence analysis was performed to ascertain the photoluminescence properties of the synthesized CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs (from sucrose) and Ru@CQDs catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: To generate CQDs from sucrose using a hydrothermal process, distilled water and a specific amount of sucrose are first placed in a pressurized reactor and heated in an oven. In the production of CQDs and Ru@CQDs, varying amounts of Ru (0.05–0.3 mmol) were investigated while keeping the sucrose amount constant at 5 mmol. Hydrothermal treatment was conducted at various temperatures (120, 150, 165, and 180 °C) and different baking durations (2, 4, 8, and 16 h). Baking time of 8 h and 150 °C temperature were determined as optimum parameters for the hydrothermal process. Once it was determined which wavelength of UV light absorbed light the best, CQDs synthesis was carried out accordingly, and then the adsorption of CQDs in a UV Fluorescent Spectrophotometer was observed. The produced Suc-CQDs were filtered through a 0.45 μm filter to remove impurities, after centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 1 h.

The Ru catalyst was loaded with CQDs using hydrothermal and impregnation techniques. The impregnation method was chosen for loading CQDs onto Ru due to its greater catalytic impact compared to the hydrothermal approach. For the loading of CQDs onto the Ru catalyst, Ru metal ratios of 0.05–0.5 mmol were used. These were combined with CQDs synthesized from 2 mmol of sucrose precursor, as CQDs themselves do not possess a fixed molar mass for direct molar quantification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NaBH4/KBH4 hydrolysis and reusability of catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: In these experiments, the optimal conditions for hydrogen production via the synthesized catalyst, in addition to the ideal conditions for the hydrothermal process, were established. The volume of hydrogen produced over time was analyzed using the principles of water and gas displacement. The hydrolysis setup included a magnetic stirrer to maintain a consistent experimental path and a circulating water bath to regulate the ambient temperature. The experimental configuration consisted of a jacketed tube, and the amount of gas released over time was measured using a gas burette. Consequently, the average hydrogen generation rate (HGR), expressed as ml min−1 g−1 cat, was used to evaluate the catalytic effectiveness of the catalyst.

HGR was calculated as shown in the equation below (Equ.3)
(3)

)

( g )
The variables in the equation are: V is the total volume of hydrogen; r is the rate; mcat is the catalyst quantity; and t is the time.

Two distinct approaches were used to load support material to the Ru catalyst. The hydrothermal process and impregnation are these techniques. After determining the support loading technique and hydrothermal conditions, the optimum conditions for the hydrolysis reactions were determined. NaOH/KOH concentration, catalyst amount, NaBH4/KBH4 concentration, and temperature parameters were measured for hydrolysis applications. The concentrations of NaOH/KOH solutions were studied as 1 %, 2.5 %, 5 %, 7.5 %, and 10 % in aqueous medium. The amount of catalyst (containing Ru amounts ranging from 0.6 to 2.56 mg) used varies between 0.24 mg and 30 mg. NaBH4/KBH4 ratios ranging from 1 % to 8 % by mass were used to find the ideal value of the NaBH4/KBH4 concentration. Studies at 30, 40, 50, and 60 °C were conducted to find the ideal temperature for hydrolysis. Furthermore, the reaction kinetics could be investigated via the data gathered from the hydrolysis studies conducted at various temperatures.

The reaction vessel was filled with catalyst and the appropriate amounts of KOH or NaOH before starting the experiment. The reaction vessel's cover was then closed, and it was submerged in the water bath. The reaction was started by adding the determined amounts of KBH4/NaBH4 to the reaction medium. The quantity of hydrogen generated was measured with a scaled burette.

The Turnover frequency (TOF) was calculated using data from catalytic reactions. This calculation was based on the amount of Ru in the catalyst used during the hydrolysis process and the volume of H2 released over time. TOF values were determined using Equation (4), incorporating data that was adjusted according to the reaction time.
(4)
T O F =

Reusability tests for the catalyst were conducted six times in succession. After an interval of 15 min between experiments, fresh NaBH4 or KBH4 was introduced, and the procedure was repeated.
NaBH4 contains 10.8 % hydrogen and KBH4 contains 7.4 % hydrogen (by mass).
The amount of hydrogen contained in 1 g of NaBH4: 1 g NaBH4 × 0.106 = 0.108 g H.

The amount of hydrogen contained in 1 g of KBH4: 1 g KBH4 × 0.074 = 0.074 g H

HPCA metric was calculated by using Equation (5).

Synthesis of CQDs from sucrose subjected to hydrothermal treatment confirmed with UV light. The appearance of carbon quantum dots produced from sucrose under daylight and UV light is given in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1(a) presents the UV–Vis spectra of CQDs and Ru@CQDs, where the sucrose-derived CQDs show a maximum absorption peak at 284 nm, attributed to the π–π∗ transitions of conjugated C=C bonds. This peak shifts to 256 nm in the Ru@CQDs spectrum, accompanied by a hypochromic and hypsochromic (blue shift) effect, likely resulting from interactions between Ru and the CQDs, changes in the electronic structure, and reduction in conjugation. In Fig. 1(b), the UV-Fluorescence spectrum is shown to further characterize the photoluminescence properties of sucrose-derived CQDs. The CQD spectrum exhibits a single broad peak across a wide band range, while the Ru@CQDs spectrum displays a narrowed and heterogeneous distribution due to Ru atoms adhering to the CQD surface, which reduces electron transitions and alters fluorescence behavior.

The TEM pictures of the Suc-CQDs and the Ru@Suc-CQDs (in pure water, 50 nm) are displayed in Figs. 2 and 3
. The TEM picture provides insight into the catalyst's homogenous structure. There is an important factor that affects the active and efficient nature of supported catalysts, which is the size of the metal particles [46]. The average particle size of sucrose-derived CQDs was 17.2 nm, while that of Ru@CQDs decreased significantly to 4.3 nm. Several factors may explain this reduction: (i) Ru metal may influence the growth kinetics of CQDs, leading to slower growth and smaller final particle sizes; (ii) Ru atoms can stabilize smaller particles by interacting strongly with carbon atoms, thus preventing aggregation; (iii) Ru metal may reduce the surface energy of CQDs, enhancing the formation of smaller, more stable nanoparticles; (iv) Ru atoms may act as a passivating layer, inhibiting further growth of the carbon dots; and (v) Ru metal inclusion has the potential to cause lattice strain in CQDs, which could also contribute to the formation of smaller particles. Nurunnabi and Turn [47] demonstrated that metal additives, including Ru, can alter pore structures and strain in supported catalysts, leading to changes in particle size. Li et al. [48] showed that incorporating CQDs into Ru-based electrocatalysts improved hydrogen evolution by facilitating the formation of smaller, well-dispersed nanoparticles. Mozaffari et al. [49] provided kinetic modeling evidence showing how metal–ligand interactions can regulate nanoparticle nucleation and growth, thus controlling size. Similarly, Li et al. [50] developed carbon quantum dot-loaded Ru nanoparticles and attributed their high catalytic activity to the reduced particle size and uniform dispersion achieved through CQD support.

Fig. 4 displays the FT-IR spectra of the Ru and Ru@CQDs catalysts. The broad and intense absorption band observed at 3100-3700 cm−1 region in the FT-IR spectrum corresponds to O–H stretching vibrations, which arise from the abundant hydroxyl groups in the sucrose-derived CQDs structure and are attributed to extensive hydrogen bonding typically occurring in this region. [51]. This broad band merged with the C-H peaks at 2932 cm−1 after the Ru-CQDs incorporation [52]. Although metallic ruthenium does not give strong signals in the FT-IR spectrum in its pure form, oxidation or adsorption products formed on the surface cause the appearance of distinct peaks in the spectrum. Following the addition of CQDs, the peaks in the Ru(0) spectrum between 1800 and 2400 cm−1 have been substantially disappeared. Additional peaks around 1701 cm−1 correspond to C-O stretching vibrations, which are indicative of carbonyl groups [53]. Additionally, the structure of the catalyst is confirmed by the peaks corresponding to the confirmation of M-C (1397 cm−1) and Ru-CQD (1099 cm−1) bonds [54].

The influence of the metal loading method using various Ru mole ratios (0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.5 mmol Ru) against a supply of 2 mmol CQDs was examined for the Ru@Suc-CQDs catalyst. Both hydrothermal heating and impregnation methods were utilized in a water environment. The hydrothermal approach is represented by the notation “MOF” in Fig. 5 , while the impregnation method is indicated by “DOT.” As shown in Fig. 5, optimization of this catalyst was achieved using the impregnation approach, as the hydrolysis reactions conducted with a 0.1 mmol Ru ratio and the catalyst synthesized by this method produced the highest hydrogen generation rate (HGR) value. Several factors may explain the increased activity of metal-loaded catalysts when using the impregnation method instead of the hydrothermal method. The impregnation technique typically promotes a higher dispersion of metal particles on the catalyst surface, which increases the number of accessible active sites and enhances catalytic performance. Additionally, it often results in smaller and more uniformly distributed nanoparticles, which are generally more catalytically active than the larger or unevenly distributed particles produced by hydrothermal methods. According to Yang et al. [55], catalysts prepared via hydrothermal impregnation exhibited superior hydrotreating performance due to enhanced dispersion of metal species. Catita et al. [56] further demonstrated that optimized control of the impregnation step—evaluated using magnetic resonance imaging and mathematical modeling—contributed to more uniform catalyst distribution and improved activity. Li et al. [57] proposed a novel impregnation–deposition method that preserved the porosity of the support while improving metal anchoring, resulting in higher catalytic efficiency for hydrodesulfurization applications. Similarly, Lee [58] emphasized that impregnation offers better control over particle distribution and metal loading uniformity, making it more effective for preparing reproducible and stable heterogeneous catalysts. Moreover, the ability to control key synthesis parameters such as temperature, pH, and metal precursor concentration during the impregnation process allows for the optimization of catalyst structure and performance.

Following the identification of the impregnation technique as the metal loading technique, the impact of various metal ratios (0.05, 0.1, 0.2, and 0.3 mmol Ru) on hydrolysis reactions was investigated based on SBH hydrolysis parameters (Fig. 6 ). The catalytic hydrolysis data in the figure indicate that the optimal incorporation value of Ru-CQDs is 0.1 mmol Ru per 5 mmol sucrose. The TEM images in Fig. 3(a–d) clearly illustrate the correlation between the incorporation amounts and the high catalytic effectiveness associated with the average particle size. The maximum HGR value was obtained when 0.1 mmol of Ru metal was used in the hydrolysis reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative analysis of optimum hydrolysis parameters and reaction kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal solution medium was determined to be pure water based on hydrolysis experiments conducted at various NaOH and KOH concentrations (0 %, 1 %, and 2.5 %) to identify the medium with the highest catalytic impact (Fig. 7 ). As shown in Fig. 7, the highest hydrogen generation rates (HGR) were achieved with 0 % NaOH or KOH, indicating that pure water is the most effective medium. One of the key desirable criteria for a catalyst is to demonstrate strong catalytic activity without the presence of NaOH or KOH, as various contaminants in the fuel cell during hydrolysis can lead to detrimental oxidation reactions [59].

The impact of the quantity of Ru in the catalyst on the efficiency of NaBH4/KBH4 hydrolysis reactions was investigated through experiments. In the NaBH4 hydrolysis tests, the maximum hydrogen production was achieved with a catalyst containing 0.96 mg Ru (160532 mL g−1min−1), while the KBH4 hydrolysis studies showed maximum production with a catalyst containing 1.2 mg Ru (85078 mL/g.min) (Fig. 8 ). The hydrolysis of PBH requires greater activation energy and may involve steps necessitating a larger catalyst surface area or more active metal centers (Ru atoms) to efficiently catalyze the reaction. In contrast to SBH hydrolysis, PBH hydrolysis may demand different interactions with the catalyst surface. By optimizing these surface contacts, the presence of additional Ru atoms can further accelerate the reaction. These factors likely explain the need for catalysts with higher Ru content in PBH hydrolysis [60].

The hydrogen production volumes achieved through hydrolysis at NaBH4/KBH4 concentrations ranging from 1 % to 8 % are presented in Fig. 9 . The hydrolysis procedure was conducted at various concentrations of NaBH4/KBH4, and the outcomes demonstrated that using 4 % NaBH4 and 4 % KBH4 produced the most hydrogen (Fig. 9). As the concentration increased from 1 % to 4 %, the hydrogen generation rate (HGR) also rose. However, further increasing the concentration to 8 % led to a decline in HGR. This decrease is attributed to the formation of highly hydrated byproducts at higher concentrations, which hinder mass transfer to the catalyst surface. Moreover, the increased solution viscosity at these concentrations significantly slows down the reaction rate [61].

The maximum HGR value of NaBH4 hydrolysis determined at 30 °C with 4 % NaBH4 is 167588 mL/g.min, whereas the maximum HGR value obtained via KBH4 hydrolysis with 4 % KBH4 is 122527 mL/g.min. These HGR values suggest that NaBH4 hydrolysis produces hydrogen at a faster rate than KBH4 hydrolysis. However, when the HGR values are adjusted based on the hydrogen content present in H sources (HPCA metric for SBH is 1551741 mL/min g and for PBH it is 1655770 mL/min g), it becomes clear that KBH4 hydrolysis releases a greater total amount of hydrogen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of temperature on the hydrolysis of NaBH4/KBH4
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetics of the NaBH4/KBH4 hydrolysis reaction were investigated at various temperatures (30, 40, 50 and 60 °C) and the related data are given in Fig. 10 . As demonstrated in Fig. 10, for both hydrolysis reactions, the initial rates of hydrogen production rose dramatically while the production time of hydrogen decreased with higher temperatures.

HGR and TOF values calculated from the hydrogen yield obtained according to different NaBH4/KBH4 concentrations are given in Table 1 . The TOF value increased as the concentration of NaBH4/KBH4 rose from 1 % to 4 %, but it reduced as the concentration of NaBH4/KBH4 rose to 8 %. This is because when the NaBH4/KBH4 concentration is high, a more hydrated byproduct is formed as a result of the reaction, which prevents mass transfer to the surface of the catalyst. As a result, the reaction rate decreases and the solution viscosity increases significantly [61].

The ln k vs. 1/T plots (Arrhenius equation) [62] obtained using the Ru@Suc-CQD catalyst at varying temperatures for NaBH4 and KBH4 hydrolysis, respectively, are displayed in Fig. 11 a and b. From the data integrated into the Arrhenius equation, the activation energy for NaBH4 hydrolysis was determined to be 38.18 kJ mol−1, while for KBH4 hydrolysis, it was found to be 39.18 kJ mol−1.

The values of (ΔS≠) and (ΔH≠) for the hydrolysis of NaBH4 were calculated using the Eyring-Polanyi equation [63], yielding ΔS≠ = 33.84 J mol−1 and ΔH≠ = 40.82 kJ mol−1. For the KBH4 hydrolysis reaction, the (ΔH≠) value was computed as 36.54 kJ mol−1, while the (ΔS≠) was determined to be 41.72 J mol−1. The lower enthalpy and entropy changes observed for NaBH4 hydrolysis, compared to KBH4 hydrolysis, can be attributed to factors such as lattice energies, cation-catalyst interactions, and variations in ionic radii and hydration energies [64]. The Na+ ion, due to its smaller size and higher charge density, interacts more strongly with water, potentially enhancing the catalytic efficiency of hydrolysis and reducing the overall energy changes of the reaction [65,66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The reusability of Ru@Suc-CQDs on hydrolysis of NaBH4 and KBH4
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrogen volume and percentage of hydrogen conversion obtained from hydrolysis reactions using the Ru@Suc-CQDs catalyst during six successive reusability experiments on NaBH4 and KBH4 hydrolysis are presented in Fig. 12 a and b. The results demonstrate that the homogeneous catalyst maintains a high catalytic performance even after six uses, achieving a 100 % hydrogen conversion rate in each cycle. Notably, after the fourth use, it was observed that the catalyst began to aggregate; however, this heterogeneity did not negatively impact its catalytic function. This observation leads to the novel concept that effective homogeneous catalysts can acquire heterogeneity through repeated use, allowing them to be removed from the reaction environment without loss of activity. It is known that the formation of metaborates during the sequential use of homogeneous catalysts negatively impacts catalytic performance, as complete isolation of the catalyst is often not achievable. This accumulation of metaborates may be one of the primary reasons for the observed decrease in catalytic efficiency with repeated use [67].

Although many studies have been conducted on supported Ru catalysts, no HGR value as high as the one obtained in this study has been reported for SBH and PBH hydrolysis. Another unique feature of the study is that this high efficiency is achieved with very low amounts of Ru and a highly cost-effective sucrose. Table 2 shows the HGR and Ea values obtained as a result of the use of some Ru-based catalysts in hydrolysis reactions. Consequently, it can be concluded that the catalyst synthesized in this investigation is efficient in catalyzing the hydrolysis reaction of potassium and sodium boron hydride.

While the processes by which KBH4 and NaBH4 hydrolyze to produce hydrogen are essentially similar, there are some variations depending on the cation (Na+ and K+). The hydrolysis reaction in the presence of water for both NaBH4 and KBH4 can be summarized by the general equation that follows:
(6) MBH4 + 2H2O → MBO2 + 4H2
Where M is either Na or K.

While NaBH4 dissociates into Na+ and BH4− ions, KBH4 dissociates into K+ and BH4− ions. Intermediate species develop as a result of the BH4 − anion being attacked by water molecules. In this reaction, water attacks the boron atom nucleophilically, releasing hydrogen gas as a consequence. The initial nucleophilic attack forms a hydroxyborane intermediate:
(7) BH4 − + H2O →BH3OH− + H2

This step is followed by further hydrolysis:
(8) BH3OH− + H2O → BH2(OH)2− + H2
(9) BH2(OH)2 − + H2O→ BH(OH)3− + H2
(10) BH(OH)3− + H2O → B(OH)4− + H2
The final product is a tetrahydroxyborate anion (B(OH)4 −). Combining with the cation, the final products are sodium borate (NaBO2) or potassium borate (KBO2) in solution [77].

Here, the effects of the cations on the mechanism will differ. Stronger interactions with water molecules (greater hydration energy) and may be the production of a more organized solvation shell can result from the Na cation's lower ionic radius and higher charge density. Both the reaction's kinetics and the stability of the intermediates may be impacted by these variables. Additionally, a quicker hydrolysis process could result from NaBH4's general higher solubility in water.

The economic feasibility of the Ru@Suc-CQDs catalyst is a critical consideration for future large-scale applications. In this study, sucrose—a widely available and inexpensive biowaste-derived carbon source—was used to produce CQDs via a hydrothermal process, minimizing the use of hazardous chemicals and high energy input. The synthesis approach does not require complex purification or separation steps, further reducing processing costs.

Although ruthenium is a noble metal, the catalyst contains a very small Ru loading (as low as 0.05–0.5 mmol), which significantly reduces the overall metal cost per gram of catalyst. (The best value for catalyst optimization was determined to be 0.1 mmol of Ru metal per 2 mmol of sucrose. This means that 5 mol% of Ru metal is used. While sucrose has a molar mass of 342.3 g/mol, RuCl3 has a molar mass of 207.4 g/mol. Thus, the Ru content is much lower in mass.) Based on commercial prices, the estimated material cost for producing 1 g of Ru@Suc-CQDs catalyst (at 0.1 mmol Ru loading) is approximately $3.80–$5.20 USD, depending on reagent source and purity. This is considerably more affordable than comparable systems using platinum or palladium.

The high catalytic efficiency (TOF, HGR) and strong reusability (up to 6 cycles without loss in activity) enhance the cost-performance ratio, making this catalyst a promising candidate for practical hydrogen generation from borohydride hydrolysis.

CQDs from sucrose were synthesized using the hydrothermal technique. Ru metal was successfully loaded into the synthesized CQDs, confirmed by UV light (360 nm) and UV fluorescence spectrophotometry. Hydrogen was generated using the Ru@Suc-CQD catalyst in the hydrolysis reactions of both SBH and PBH. The optimum conditions for SBH hydrolysis using the Ru@Suc-CQD catalyst are as follows: metal loading via the impregnation method, 0.1 mmol Ru metal ratio, pure water solution medium, catalyst containing 0.96 mg Ru, and 4 % NaBH4 concentration. The HGR value measured under these conditions is 167588 mL min−1 g−1. The TOF, Ea, ΔH, and ΔS values for SBH hydrolysis were measured as 924.8 molH2.molRu−1.min−1, 38.18 kJ mol−1, 40.82 kJ mol−1, and -33.84 J mol−1, respectively. The optimum conditions for hydrogen production of KBH4 hydrolysis catalyzed by a homogeneous catalyst (Ru@Suc-CQDs) are as follows: metal loading by impregnation method, 0.1 mmol Ru metal ratio, pure water solution medium, catalyst containing 1.2 mg Ru, and 4 % KBH4 concentration. The HGR value measured under these conditions is 122527 mL min−1 g−1. The TOF, Ea, ΔH, and ΔS values for KBH4 hydrolysis were measured as 573.3 molH2.molRu−1.min−1, 39.18 kJ mol−1, 36.54 kJ mol−1, and -41.72 J mol−1, respectively.

A novel metric—HPCA—was proposed to evaluate catalyst performance based on the actual hydrogen content of the borohydride source. The HPCA value for PBH (1.655,770 mL g−1 min−1) was found to be higher than that of SBH (1.551,741 mL g−1 min−1) indicating that PBH may be a more efficient hydrogen donor under optimized conditions.

The Ru@Suc-CQDs catalyst also demonstrated excellent stability, maintaining full catalytic activity over six consecutive cycles. These results highlight the potential of biomass-derived CQDs as cost-effective and sustainable supports for noble metal-based hydrogen production catalysts.
